bins.name: State Retail Jurisdiction Protected
bins.desc: NESCOE argues the proposed ANOPR encroaches on state authority over retail electricity sales, which the FPA reserves to states. FERC has no clear congressional authorization to assert jurisdiction over large load interconnections under either the FPA or the major questions doctrine, and the DOE letter provides no legal support for a change in approach.
quotes: the ANOPR, as proposed, would encroach on the exclusive jurisdiction of the states to regulate retail customers per the provisions of the Federal Power Act ("FPA"), contrary to the Commission's longstanding tradition and recent actions recognizing the states' jurisdiction in this area.
NESCOE recommends that the Commission not proceed with the ANOPR as proposed by DOE.
The FPA establishes a clear division: states oversee retail electricity services while FERC oversees interstate transmission and wholesale electricity markets.
Historically, FERC has deferred to the states on large load interconnections, viewing them as part of the local distribution system and related to end-user retail sales, even if the load is large.

if the Commission were to assert exclusive jurisdiction over the interconnection of large load end-use retail customers to the transmission system, it would be exercising federal jurisdiction over retail delivery service, which has historically been the role of the states.

the proposed ANOPR would assert authority over the interconnection of large loads such as data centers with little to no role for the states, exceeding the statutory jurisdiction of the states under the FPA as traditionally recognized by the Commission.
The DOE Letter itself identifies no legal support for FERC's authority to change its approach to load interconnections.

dissenting opinions do not represent binding legal authority, and thus the Commission should not rely on this language to assert jurisdiction that Congress has not expressly conferred upon it by statute.

In order to proceed with the ANOPR as proposed, per the major questions doctrine, FERC is required to point to clear and specific permission from Congress that would allow it to assert jurisdiction over large load interconnection.

bins.name: State Tariff Costs and Stranded Risk
bins.desc: At least 20 states already have large load tariffs that address reliability and cost allocation, including financial commitments and curtailment protocols. The ANOPR as proposed would create confusion about these programs, potentially strand costs for existing customers, and disrupt projects already underway.
quotes: at least 20 states have approved or have pending large load tariffs or similar measures, which may include financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms to allow for the rapid interconnection of large loads without compromising grid reliability or unduly burdening existing retail customers

The ANOPR, as proposed, would create confusion as to the effect of these efforts and could compromise reliability, disrupt existing projects, create stranded costs for customers, and invite otherwise unnecessary legal challenges.

bins.name: Regional Reliability Differences Matter
bins.desc: Uniform national interconnection rules risk pushing already constrained regions past their reliability limits. NESCOE notes that data center load growth varies widely by region, and New England has relatively low penetration compared to other areas, making a one-size-fits-all rule particularly ill-suited.
quotes: Standards and processes that some regions can make work might drive already over-subscribed regions past the breaking point and cause significant harm to reliability.

bins.name: ISO-NE Region Constraints
bins.desc: New England has low data center interconnection levels compared to other regions, but the region is already constrained. NESCOE warns that standards designed for other regions could harm reliability in ISO-NE and similar over-subscribed areas.
quotes: the ANOPR, as currently proposed, takes a cookie-cutter approach that could result in unintended impacts due to differences between states and regions.
Standards and processes that some regions can make work might drive already over-subscribed regions past the breaking point and cause significant harm to reliability.

bins.name: FPA Federal-State Divide
bins.desc: The FPA draws a clear line between FERC jurisdiction over wholesale and interstate transmission and state jurisdiction over retail sales and local distribution. NESCOE contends the proposed ANOPR crosses that line by asserting federal control over large load interconnections that have historically been treated as retail matters governed by the states.
quotes: the ANOPR, as proposed, would encroach on the exclusive jurisdiction of the states to regulate retail customers per the provisions of the Federal Power Act ("FPA"), contrary to the Commission's longstanding tradition and recent actions recognizing the states' jurisdiction in this area.
NESCOE recommends that the Commission not proceed with the ANOPR as proposed by DOE.
The FPA establishes a clear division: states oversee retail electricity services while FERC oversees interstate transmission and wholesale electricity markets.
Historically, FERC has deferred to the states on large load interconnections, viewing them as part of the local distribution system and related to end-user retail sales, even if the load is large.

if the Commission were to assert exclusive jurisdiction over the interconnection of large load end-use retail customers to the transmission system, it would be exercising federal jurisdiction over retail delivery service, which has historically been the role of the states.

the proposed ANOPR would assert authority over the interconnection of large loads such as data centers with little to no role for the states, exceeding the statutory jurisdiction of the states under the FPA as traditionally recognized by the Commission.

bins.name: Inadequate Legal Basis for ANOPR
bins.desc: The DOE letter and proposed ANOPR supply no sufficient legal justification for FERC to depart from its historic deference to states. The ANOPR improperly relies on a Supreme Court dissent, not binding authority, and the major questions doctrine requires clear and specific congressional authorization that the FPA does not provide.
quotes: The DOE Letter itself identifies no legal support for FERC's authority to change its approach to load interconnections.

dissenting opinions do not represent binding legal authority, and thus the Commission should not rely on this language to assert jurisdiction that Congress has not expressly conferred upon it by statute.

In order to proceed with the ANOPR as proposed, per the major questions doctrine, FERC is required to point to clear and specific permission from Congress that would allow it to assert jurisdiction over large load interconnection.

bins.name: State and Regional Differences
bins.desc: Large load growth rates and existing regulatory frameworks differ substantially by state and region. A cookie-cutter federal rule ignores this variation and could cause serious unintended consequences, particularly for regions that are already over-subscribed or that have already developed tailored state tariffs.
quotes: the ANOPR, as currently proposed, takes a cookie-cutter approach that could result in unintended impacts due to differences between states and regions.
Standards and processes that some regions can make work might drive already over-subscribed regions past the breaking point and cause significant harm to reliability.

at least 20 states have approved or have pending large load tariffs or similar measures, which may include financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms to allow for the rapid interconnection of large loads without compromising grid reliability or unduly burdening existing retail customers

The ANOPR, as proposed, would create confusion as to the effect of these efforts and could compromise reliability, disrupt existing projects, create stranded costs for customers, and invite otherwise unnecessary legal challenges.

bins.name: Existing State Programs at Risk
bins.desc: At least 20 states have approved or pending large load tariffs. The proposed ANOPR would create legal uncertainty about those programs, risk stranding costs for customers, and disrupt projects that are already in progress under state oversight.
quotes: at least 20 states have approved or have pending large load tariffs or similar measures, which may include financial commitments, curtailment protocols, and minimum contract terms to allow for the rapid interconnection of large loads without compromising grid reliability or unduly burdening existing retail customers

The ANOPR, as proposed, would create confusion as to the effect of these efforts and could compromise reliability, disrupt existing projects, create stranded costs for customers, and invite otherwise unnecessary legal challenges.

bins.name: DOE Six-Month Deadline Unreasonable
bins.desc: DOE requests a final rule by April 30, 2026, fewer than six months away. NESCOE compares this to prior complex rulemakings (Order 2023 took 13 months; Order 1920 took nearly three years) and argues the timeline is not reasonable under Section 403 of the FPA, which requires only that FERC abide by reasonable time limits.
quotes: the DOE Letter requests that the Commission take "final action" no later than April 30, 2026, which is less than six months from now.
issuance of a final rule by FERC within the next six months as DOE requests is not reasonable.
